text:
Leaving home for the first time and feeling that ache is one of the most ordinary and most human things there is, even though right now it probably feels like something only you are carrying. What you're feeling isn't weakness or a sign you've made a mistake. It's just love with nowhere to land at the moment, the proof of how much your home and the people in it actually mean to you. People who have somewhere they miss are lucky, even when the missing hurts.

It helps to remember that homesickness isn't a permanent state, it's a passage. The sharpest part tends to come early, when everything around you is unfamiliar and your mind keeps reaching for the old rhythms that aren't there anymore. As the new place slowly becomes known to you, as you find a coffee shop you like or a familiar face or a path you can walk without thinking, the ache loosens. It doesn't mean you'll forget home or stop loving it. It just means your heart makes room for more than one place at a time.

Be gentle with yourself in the meantime. Call the people you miss, but don't only live in those calls. Let yourself feel the sadness without deciding it means anything bigger than it does. Try to do one small ordinary thing each day that puts you out in your new world, even when you'd rather stay curled up. And keep one or two comforts from home nearby, a song, a recipe, a photo, because carrying a piece of where you came from isn't clinging to the past, it's how you build a bridge to where you are.

You're going to be okay. The fact that you can miss something this much is a sign of how deeply you're able to belong, and that same capacity is exactly what will help you build a sense of home right where you are now. Give it a little time, and be kind to the version of you that's still finding its footing.
